title: Roast turkey crown
subtitle: Boneless breast for smaller numbers, 90 minutes
excerpt: A turkey crown is the breast still attached to the breastbone, with the legs removed. Faster than a whole turkey, easier to carve, and more reliably juicy. Best for Christmas tables of four to six.
sourceNotes: Turkey crown became a mainstream British Christmas product from the 1990s as smaller households became more common. The method is a simplified version of the whole turkey roast.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Apply the herb butter up to 24 hours ahead and refrigerate uncovered for crisper skin.

glossaryTerms.definition: Spooning the pan juices over the crown during roasting to keep the breast moist and promote even browning across the skin.
Leaving the cooked turkey crown to stand before carving. 30 minutes is sufficient and keeps the bird hot for a long time due to its mass.

content.text: A turkey crown feeds four to six people comfortably without the logistics of a whole turkey. The breastbone still present gives structure; without legs attached, the cooking time drops to 90 minutes for a 2 kg crown. The main risk is the same as with a whole turkey: a dry breast. The butter under the skin addresses this directly.

items.prepNote: to cover the breast during cooking

content.text: Remove the crown from the fridge 1 hour before cooking. Heat the oven to 190 degrees C fan. Mix the softened butter with the thyme, garlic, lemon zest, and salt. Carefully loosen the skin over the breast and push the herb butter underneath, spreading it as evenly as possible.
Season the skin with extra salt. If using bacon, lay it across the top of the crown in overlapping layers. Place in a roasting tin.
Roast for 1 hour 30 minutes for a 2 kg crown,
every 30 minutes. Remove the bacon for the last 20 minutes to bronze the breast. Check that the thickest part of the breast reads 75 degrees C.
for 30 minutes loosely covered with foil before carving. Carve down alongside the breastbone on each side and slice across the grain.

items.symptom: The breast is overcooked and dry
items.cause: The crown was left in beyond 75 degrees C, or was not basted.
items.fix: Use a thermometer and remove at exactly 75 degrees C. Baste every 30 minutes. The bacon cover also protects the breast significantly.

items.symptom: The skin is pale after removing the bacon
items.cause: Only 10 to 15 minutes with the bacon off rather than 20.
items.fix: Give the skin a full 20 minutes uncovered. Raise to 200 degrees C if needed for the final 10 minutes.

items.symptom: The pan juices have reduced to a dark sticky residue
items.cause: The crown was not basted and the juices cooked down too fast.
items.fix: Add 100 ml of hot water or stock to the tin and stir to lift the residue. Use this as the base for gravy.

items.symptom: The butter has leaked from under the skin
items.cause: The butter was not worked far enough under the skin, or the skin was too loose.
items.fix: Push the butter as far as possible into the corners of the crown under the skin. Some leakage is normal; most should stay in place.

content.text: A cranberry and orange butter instead of herb butter gives a festive finish. For a richer result, use a clementine and sage butter. The crown can also be stuffed between the skin and breast with a thin layer of sage and onion stuffing.

The turkey crown emerged as a mainstream British product in the 1990s, designed for households of three or four who found a whole turkey too large and too much work. Supermarkets drove its popularity by selling crowns pre-tied and butter-basted. For many smaller British households it is now the standard Christmas centrepiece, producing a neater and faster result than the whole bird.
